coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_only: Use a per-tenant token bucket sharded across the gateway nodes, with Redis holding the shared counters. Give each tenant a configured requests-per-second with a burst allowance, and apply GCRA for smooth pacing. Add a sliding window so edge bursts don't double-count. This is the standard, well-understood approach and it scales horizontally.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: fairness is per-tenant and independent. World-test: a workload where tenant A is idle and tenant B saturates a shared bottleneck; the design must lend A's unused capacity to B and reclaim it instantly when A returns. Known family it must beat: per-tenant token_bucket, which caps B at its quota even while capacity is idle. Max claim: a better fairness/utilization tradeoff under contention.
coding_rate_limiter_001.llm_greenstar: Break tenant_independent (axis OBJECTIVE): a work-conserving, COUPLED limiter that allocates the shared bottleneck by max-min fairness over currently-active tenants, instead of a fixed per-tenant quota. New output: a per-tick allocation that depends on the active set. Which family can't: per-tenant token_bucket wastes idle capacity under skew. World-test: construct a 2-tenant workload where A is idle 50% and B is saturated; the coupled limiter gives B the idle capacity and reclaims it for A on return, beating the per-tenant baseline on utilization at equal tail-fairness. Negative control: shuffle the coupling (allocate independently) and the utilization gain must collapse to the token_bucket baseline. Max claim: a strictly better utilization-vs-fairness frontier under contention; needs production traces.

coding_cache_002.llm_only: Partition the cache per tenant with size quotas and use TinyLFU admission to keep high-value entries. Per-tenant quotas stop one tenant from evicting another's hot set, while TinyLFU protects frequently-used keys. Back it with a sharded store and per-tenant LRU within each partition.
coding_cache_002.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: cache capacity is statically partitioned per tenant. World-test: a bursty workload where one tenant's working set spikes while another is idle; the cache must lend idle capacity then reclaim it. Known family it must beat: partitioned LRU / TinyLFU with static quotas. Max claim: better aggregate hit-rate under skew at a fairness floor.
coding_cache_002.llm_greenstar: Break the static-partition assumption (axis DECOMPOSITION): a work-conserving shared cache that allocates capacity by marginal hit-rate gain (live miss-curves) across active tenants. New output: per-tenant capacity that tracks each tenant's miss curve. Which family can't: static partitioned LRU wastes idle capacity. World-test: construct a workload where A's working set is idle and B's spikes; the elastic cache lends A's space to B and reclaims on A's return, beating partitioned LRU on aggregate hit-rate at an equal fairness floor. Negative control: freeze the allocation (static) and the hit-rate gain must collapse to partitioned LRU. Max claim: a better hit-rate/fairness frontier under burstiness; needs per-tenant miss-curve estimates.

coding_backpressure_003.llm_only: Use bounded per-tenant queues with credit-based flow control. When the downstream saturates, propagate backpressure by withholding credits upstream. Weight each request's credit cost so expensive requests consume more of the budget, keeping the queue length bounded and latency predictable.
coding_backpressure_003.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: every request costs the same. World-test: a workload mixing cheap and 100x-cost requests; the scheme must throttle by actual downstream work, not by request count. Known family it must beat: length-based bounded queues. Max claim: correct throttling under heterogeneous cost.
coding_backpressure_003.llm_greenstar: Break cost_uniform (axis COST_MODEL): meter backpressure by downstream WORK occupancy (cost-weighted in-flight), not by queue length. New output: admission gated on cost-weighted occupancy. Which family can't: length-based bounded queues mis-throttle when costs are skewed. World-test: construct a workload mixing 1x and 100x-cost requests where length-based backpressure lets a heavy-cost tenant saturate the downstream while appearing under quota; cost-weighted occupancy throttles it correctly and protects the others. Negative control: set all costs equal and the scheme must reduce to length-based backpressure. Max claim: correct throttling under heterogeneous cost; needs per-request cost estimates.

coding_scheduler_004.llm_only: Use a bounded concurrency limiter (a semaphore) per tenant sized to its SLA, plus a global cap. Admit a request only when in-flight < limit; otherwise queue briefly and shed on overflow. Concurrency limiting naturally tracks load better than request-count limiting under variable durations.
coding_scheduler_004.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: rate is measured over time windows. World-test: bursty arrivals where window limiting over-admits at window edges while concurrency limiting tracks real in-flight load. Known family it must beat: sliding-window / token bucket. Max claim: steadier utilization under burstiness.
coding_scheduler_004.llm_greenstar: Break time_windowed (axis REPRESENTATION): admit on in-flight CONCURRENCY (work in progress) rather than arrivals-per-window. New output: an admission rule on concurrent occupancy. Which family can't: window limiters over-admit at edges or under-utilize mid-window. World-test: construct a bursty workload with variable request durations where window limiting over-admits at the window edge; concurrency-based admission tracks actual load and holds the SLA. Negative control: make all requests equal-duration with uniform arrivals and the policy must reduce to the sliding-window baseline. Max claim: steadier utilization under burstiness; not new when durations are uniform.

math_convergence_001.llm_only: For an L-smooth (possibly non-convex) objective, gradient descent with stepsize 1/L satisfies min_{t<=T} ||grad f(x_t)||^2 <= 2L(f(x_0)-f*)/T, so the smallest gradient norm converges at O(1/sqrt(T)) to a stationary point. SGD with decreasing steps achieves the same rate in expectation. This is the classical guarantee.
math_convergence_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: convergence requires global L-smoothness. World-test: a function not globally L-smooth (a quartic, curvature unbounded) where fixed-step GD diverges or must take vanishing steps. Known family it must beat: gradient_descent / Adam under L-smoothness. Max claim: convergence under a strictly weaker hypothesis on a class the smooth theory cannot cover.
math_convergence_001.llm_greenstar: Break smoothness_required (axis HYPOTHESIS): replace global L-smoothness by RELATIVE smoothness w.r.t. a reference convex h (Lh - f convex in the Bregman sense). Then mirror descent with Bregman divergence D_h and constant step 1/L gives a descent inequality and converges at O(1/T) in the Bregman stationarity measure, with NO global Lipschitz-gradient assumption. New output: a guarantee on unbounded-curvature functions (h=||x||^4). Which family can't: Euclidean GD/Adam diverge for any fixed step on a quartic. World-test: construct f(x)=||x||^4/4 (not L-smooth); Euclidean GD with fixed step diverges while Bregman/mirror descent with h=||x||^4 converges. Negative control: set h back to (1/2)||x||^2 and the method reduces to GD and must fail on the same quartic. Max claim: convergence under a weaker hypothesis on a specific class; needs a valid reference h.

math_geometry_002.llm_only: Use mirror descent with a Bregman divergence matched to the constraint geometry, e.g. the entropy mirror map on the simplex. The convergence rate then depends on the diameter in the chosen norm and the strong convexity of the mirror map, rather than on the Euclidean Lipschitz constant, which can be far better for ill-conditioned-in-Euclidean problems.
math_geometry_002.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: distance/geometry is Euclidean. World-test: a problem ill-conditioned in the Euclidean norm but well-conditioned under a non-Euclidean reference (simplex/entropy). Known family it must beat: Euclidean gradient descent. Max claim: a geometry-dependent rate on a specific class.
math_geometry_002.llm_greenstar: Break euclidean_metric (axis STRUCTURE): let the rate be governed by relative smoothness w.r.t. a reference h capturing the problem geometry, not by global Euclidean L. New output: a rate in the Bregman geometry of h. Which family can't: Euclidean GD is slow when the geometry is non-Euclidean. World-test: construct a problem ill-conditioned in Euclidean norm but well-conditioned under entropy h on the simplex; Euclidean GD is slow/diverges while mirror descent with h converges fast. Negative control: set h=(1/2)||x||^2 and the rate must reduce to the Euclidean GD baseline. Max claim: a strictly geometry-dependent rate on that class.

math_proximal_003.llm_only: Use the proximal point or proximal-gradient method. For convex f the proximal point method converges without differentiability; for composite f=g+h with g smooth and h nonsmooth, proximal gradient handles h via its proximal operator and converges at O(1/T), or O(1/T^2) with acceleration.
math_proximal_003.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: convergence needs L-smoothness of the smooth part. World-test: a composite where the smooth part is relatively-but-not-L-smooth (a quartic) and Euclidean prox-gradient needs vanishing steps. Known family it must beat: Euclidean proximal gradient. Max claim: convergence under a weaker hypothesis on a function class.
math_proximal_003.llm_greenstar: Break smoothness_required (axis HYPOTHESIS): prove convergence of proximal gradient under RELATIVE smoothness (a Bregman proximal step) without global L. New output: a guarantee for composites whose smooth part is relatively smooth w.r.t. h. Which family can't: Euclidean prox-gradient needs vanishing steps on an unbounded-curvature smooth part. World-test: construct a composite where the smooth part is f(x)=||x||^4/4; Euclidean prox-gradient with constant step diverges while Bregman prox-gradient with reference h=||x||^4 and constant step converges. Negative control: set the reference to the Euclidean (1/2)||x||^2 and it reduces to standard prox-gradient and must fail on the quartic. Max claim: convergence under a weaker-than-smoothness hypothesis on that class.

generic_unknown_001.llm_only: Use a skill rating such as Elo or TrueSkill and match players within a rating band, widening the band as queue time grows to bound wait. Add role and latency constraints. This balances fairness (close ratings) against waiting time and is the standard matchmaking design.
generic_unknown_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: fairness equals closeness in a scalar skill rating. World-test: rosters equal in mean rating but differing in predicted game closeness; the system should produce closer games, not just equal ratings. Known family it must beat: Elo/TrueSkill rating-band matching. Max claim: closer games at equal wait time.
generic_unknown_001.llm_greenstar: Break the assumption that fairness = closeness in a SCALAR rating (axis REPRESENTATION): match on predicted match-QUALITY (probability of a close game) from a multi-factor model (skill, role synergy, latency, playstyle), not scalar rating distance. New output: a match-quality predictor used as the matching objective. Which family can't: Elo-band matching ignores composition and latency. World-test: construct two rosters equal in mean rating but differing in predicted closeness; quality-matched games are closer (smaller score margin) than band-matched ones. Negative control: collapse the predictor to scalar rating and it must reduce to Elo-band matching. Max claim: closer games at equal wait; needs labeled match-outcome data to train the predictor.

self_improve_001.llm_only: Add an external benchmark that compares the library's output to baselines on held-out tasks, with metrics for novelty and usefulness, plus a readiness gate that blocks release unless it beats the baselines. Track the metrics over time to catch regressions.
self_improve_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: value equals the internal self-graded structural score. World-test: a dishonest output must score high on naive structure yet low on a value metric; if it doesn't, the metric is decorative. Known family it must beat: a single structural novelty score. Max claim: a harder-to-game, non-decorative eval.
self_improve_001.llm_greenstar: Break the assumption that value = internal structural score (axis MEASURE): measure value by a model-in-the-loop benchmark (real assistant answers scored on falsifiability and distance-from-box) plus a real-world novelty audit, with a task-type-aware primary score (judgment accuracy for assessment tasks, generative novelty for design tasks). Which family can't: a single structural VNS can be gamed and cannot credit a correct judgment. World-test: construct a dishonest OVERCLAIM mode; it must score high on naive structure but low on the honesty-gated robust score, and judge() must beat its no-judge ablation on judgment tasks. Negative control: remove the honesty gates and the gap must collapse. Max claim: a measurably non-decorative, harder-to-game eval; not yet proof of real-world superiority, which needs the model-in-the-loop run.

routing_ambiguous_001.llm_only: This is ambiguous: 'convergence' suggests numerical optimization while 'low latency' suggests systems. If optimization, use an accelerated method (Nesterov or Anderson acceleration) for fast convergence; if systems, use an approximate algorithm with early stopping under a latency budget. Absent more context I would combine them: Anderson-accelerated iteration with an early-exit latency cap.
routing_ambiguous_001.llm_checklist: Assumption breaking: convergence and latency are separate objectives. World-test: a task with a hard latency deadline where the method must return the best usable iterate by the deadline. Known family it must beat: a slow-but-accurate solver and a fast-but-crude one. Max claim: better quality-at-deadline.
routing_ambiguous_001.llm_greenstar: The prompt is ambiguous between optimization-convergence and systems-latency; break the assumption that they are separate (axis REPRESENTATION): treat them as a JOINT objective, an anytime algorithm optimizing convergence-per-millisecond that returns a usable iterate under a hard latency budget. New output: a quality-at-deadline curve. Which family can't: a pure solver ignores the deadline; a crude heuristic ignores quality. World-test: construct a workload with a strict latency deadline where the anytime method beats both a slow-but-accurate solver (misses the deadline) and a fast-but-crude one (low quality) on quality-at-deadline. Negative control: remove the deadline and it must reduce to ordinary accelerated convergence. Max claim: better quality-at-deadline; needs the latency budget specified.
